name: inception_resnet

members.name: InceptionResNetConfig
members.signature: InceptionResNetConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, dropout: float = 0.2, scale_a: float = 0.17, scale_b: float = 0.1, scale_c: float = 0.2)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, dropout: float = 0.2, scale_a: float = 0.17, scale_b: float = 0.1, scale_c: float = 0.2)

members.extended:
Uses the same stem as Inception-v4, followed by:
  - Mixed_5b (192 → 320)
  - 10× Block35  (scale_a, default 0.17)
  - Mixed_6a / Reduction-A (320 → 1088)
  - 20× Block17  (scale_b, default 0.10)
  - Mixed_7a / Reduction-B (1088 → 2080)
  -  9× Block8   (scale_c, default 0.20) + 1× Block8 (no ReLU)
  - Conv2d_7b 1×1 projection (2080 → 1536)
  - AdaptiveAvgPool → Dropout → FC(1536, num_classes)

`scale_a` — residual scale for Block35 (paper suggests 0.17).
`scale_b` — residual scale for Block17 (paper suggests 0.10).
`scale_c` — residual scale for Block8  (paper suggests 0.20).
`dropout` — head dropout rate (0.2 in the paper).

members.name: InceptionResNetOutput
members.signature: InceptionResNetOutput(logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None)
members.extended:
Inception-ResNet v2 does not use auxiliary classifiers (unlike
Inception v3 / GoogLeNet) — the residual shortcuts already mitigate
the vanishing-gradient problem that auxiliary heads were originally
introduced to address — so this dataclass only carries the main
logits and an optional loss tensor.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description: Main classifier output of shape `(B, num_classes)`.

parameters.name: loss
parameters.description:
Cross-entropy loss when labels were passed to `forward`;
`None` otherwise.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception_resnet import inception_resnet_v2_cls
>>> model = inception_resnet_v2_cls().eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
>>> out.loss is None
True

members.name: InceptionResNetV2
members.extended:
Implements the residual-Inception topology from Szegedy et al.,
"Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual
Connections on Learning", AAAI 2017.  The body consists of three
residual Inception block families — `_Block35` (×10),
`_Block17` (×20), and `_Block8` (×9 + 1 no-ReLU
block) — separated by Reduction-A (`_Mixed6a`) and
Reduction-B (`_Mixed7a`) blocks, with a leading Mixed_5b
pre-block (`_Mixed5b`) and a final $1\times1$
projection (`conv2d_7b`) to 1536 channels.  Designed for
$299\times299$ RGB inputs.  State-dict naming matches the
canonical timm / TensorFlow-Slim implementation so weight transfer
is straightforward.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `inception_resnet_v2` for
the paper-cited configuration with the recommended residual
scale factors (0.17 / 0.10 / 0.20).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception_resnet import inception_resnet_v2
>>> backbone = inception_resnet_v2()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B, 1536, 1, 1)
(1, 1536, 1, 1)
notes:
Each residual Inception block computes



$$
y = \sigma\!\left(x + \alpha \cdot \mathcal{F}_{\text{inception}}(x)\right),
$$

with $\alpha$ a small fixed scale factor (0.10–0.20).  The
residual-scale trick was introduced specifically for
Inception-ResNet because unscaled residuals caused training to
diverge on networks with many filters per Inception module.
Approximately 55.8 M parameters; achieves a top-5 ImageNet
validation error of 3.08% in the original paper.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed via
`BackboneMixin`.

members.name: InceptionResNetV2ForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: Inception-ResNet v2 image classifier (backbone + GAP + dropout + linear).
members.extended:
Combines an `InceptionResNetV2` backbone with a
global-average-pool, optional `lucid.nn.Dropout`
(`p=config.dropout`, default 0.2), and a single
`lucid.nn.Linear` projection producing
`config.num_classes` logits.  No auxiliary classifier — the
residual shortcuts already provide effective gradient flow
throughout the network.  The classifier attribute is named
`classif` (not `classifier`) to match the canonical timm /
TensorFlow-Slim state-dict key.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use `inception_resnet_v2_cls` for the
paper-cited configuration.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception_resnet import inception_resnet_v2_cls
>>> model = inception_resnet_v2_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 1000)
notes:
From Szegedy et al., "Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the
Impact of Residual Connections on Learning", AAAI 2017.  Loss is
the standard categorical cross-entropy



$$
\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}
    \log \operatorname{softmax}(\text{logits}_n)_{\,y_n},
$$

computed only when `labels` is not `None`.  Approximately
55.8 M parameters; top-5 ImageNet validation error 3.08%.  The key
empirical message of the paper is that residual connections do not
raise the final accuracy ceiling beyond a comparably-sized
non-residual Inception v4, but they *dramatically* accelerate
training convergence.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: conv2d_1a … conv2d_7b, mixed_5b, repeat, mixed_6a, repeat_1,
attributes.description:
mixed_7a, repeat_2, block8
Same backbone components as on `InceptionResNetV2`.

attributes.name: classif
attributes.description:
Final classifier projecting 1536 → `num_classes` — named
`classif` for state-dict compatibility.

members.name: inception_resnet_v2
members.extended:
Builds an `InceptionResNetV2` with the paper-cited Szegedy
2017 topology: 5-conv stem → Mixed_5b → 10× Block35 → Reduction-A
→ 20× Block17 → Reduction-B → 9× Block8 (+ 1 no-ReLU block) →
$1\times1$ projection to 1536 channels.  Designed for
$299\times299$ RGB inputs.  Approximately 54.3 M parameters
in the backbone alone.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `InceptionResNetConfig`.
Use `scale_a` / `scale_b` / `scale_c` to tune the
residual-branch scaling factors (defaults 0.17 / 0.10 / 0.20
from the paper).

returns.description:
Backbone with the Inception-ResNet v2 configuration applied
(or with `overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception_resnet import inception_resnet_v2
>>> model = inception_resnet_v2()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B, 1536, 1, 1)
(1, 1536, 1, 1)
notes:
See Szegedy et al., "Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact
of Residual Connections on Learning", AAAI 2017.  Top-5 ImageNet
validation error of 3.08% (full classifier).  No paper-cited size
variants (H11) — only v2 is implemented here; v1 differs primarily
in widths.

members.name: inception_resnet_v2_cls
members.signature: inception_resnet_v2_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: InceptionResNetV2Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Inception-ResNet v2 image classifier (backbone + GAP + dropout + linear).
members.extended:
Builds an `InceptionResNetV2ForImageClassification` with the
paper-cited Szegedy 2017 topology and a single
`lucid.nn.Linear` classifier head producing
`config.num_classes` logits.  Approximately 55.8 M parameters
total; achieves a top-5 ImageNet validation error of 3.08%.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`InceptionResNetV2Weights.TF_IN1K`);
a tag string (e.g. `"TF_IN1K"`) → that specific checkpoint.
Mutually exclusive with `weights` (which wins if both are
given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member, e.g.
`InceptionResNetV2Weights.TF_IN1K`.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `InceptionResNetConfig`.
Common picks: `num_classes=N` to retarget the head,
`dropout=p` to adjust regularisation.  Note: overriding
`num_classes` away from the checkpoint's class count makes
pretrained loading fail the strict key/shape check — load with a
matching head, then call `reset_classifier`.

returns.description:
Classifier with the Inception-ResNet v2 configuration applied
(or with `overrides` merged on top of it), optionally
initialised from pretrained weights.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception_resnet import inception_resnet_v2_cls
>>> model = inception_resnet_v2_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 1000)
Load ImageNet-pretrained weights:
>>> model = inception_resnet_v2_cls(pretrained=True)        # DEFAULT tag
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception_resnet import InceptionResNetV2Weights
>>> model = inception_resnet_v2_cls(weights=InceptionResNetV2Weights.TF_IN1K)
notes:
See Szegedy et al., "Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact
of Residual Connections on Learning", AAAI 2017, §3.3.  The
classifier attribute is named `classif` (not `classifier`) for
timm / TensorFlow-Slim state-dict compatibility.  Pretrained weights
are converted from timm's `inception_resnet_v2.tf_in1k` checkpoint
and hosted on the Hugging Face Hub under
`lucid-dl/inception-resnet-v2`.

members.name: InceptionResNetV2Weights
members.extended: Szegedy et al. AAAI 2017 (55.8 M params, top-1 80.46%).
notes:
Reference: Szegedy, Ioffe, Vanhoucke, Alemi, *"Inception-v4,
Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on
Learning"*, AAAI 2017 ([arXiv:1602.07261](https://arxiv.org/abs/1602.07261)).

attributes.name: TF_IN1K
attributes.description:
TensorFlow-Slim ImageNet-1k checkpoint (top-1 80.46% / top-5
95.31%), sourced from `timm/inception_resnet_v2.tf_in1k`.

summary: Inception-ResNet v2 family — Szegedy et al., 2016.
citation: Szegedy, Christian, et al. "Inception-v4, Inception-ResNet and the Impact of Residual Connections on Learning." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2017.
theory:
Inception-ResNet hybridises the two dominant architectural ideas
of the early ResNet era: the *multi-branch Inception module* and
the *residual shortcut*.  Each Inception sub-network is wrapped in
an identity skip connection, so the block computes



$$
y = x + \alpha \cdot \mathcal{F}_{\text{inception}}(x),
$$

where $\alpha$ is a small fixed scale factor (typically
0.10–0.30) applied to the Inception branch before addition.  This
*residual scaling* trick was introduced specifically for
Inception-ResNet because the unscaled residual branches
occasionally caused training to diverge on networks with many
filters per Inception module.

Three block families — Block35, Block17, Block8 — mirror
Inception v3's A/B/C topologies at the 35×35, 17×17, and 8×8
resolutions respectively, but each is now a residual unit.  The
network's stem is shared with Inception v4 (an aggressive
multi-branch downsampler).  Reduction-A and Reduction-B blocks
perform spatial reduction between groups of residual Inception
blocks while doubling the channel budget.

The empirical message of the paper is precise and influential:
residual connections *do not* improve final accuracy beyond what
a comparably-sized non-residual Inception v4 achieves, but they
*dramatically* accelerate convergence — Inception-ResNet v2
reaches the same accuracy roughly twice as fast.  Final top-5
error on ImageNet is 3.08%, with the residual variants holding a
convergence-speed edge over their non-residual siblings throughout
training.
